Great to meet with the [Beca](https://nz.linkedin.com/company/beca?trk=public_post-text) team in Auckland and see how they are using Azure, Foundry, and their BEYON platform to make the New Zealand Geotechnical Database more accessible and useful.

It is a powerful example of AI helping engineers access critical data faster and make better decisions, as they build more resilient infrastructure across New Zealand.

##### **Transcript**

We are a country that lives with a lot of natural process, so volcanic field, climate events, flooding, we're used to those things. Earthquakes are very sudden, very dramatic. The earthquake in Christchurch in 2011 was just shocking for the whole country. Seeing part of your backyard being so devastated in such a scale of change and lost. The Christchurch earthquake really exposed to New Zealand as a whole, our dependence on timely access to all this. Technical information that was really essential when designing buildings. We were struggling because the data wasn't collated in any way. As a result, the New Zealand government set up the New Zealand Geotechnical Database to make it easier to access this information. As it grew, it became much more difficult to scale and make available in a standardized way. Here at Baker, we saw an opportunity for us to build something that was going to support the community and infrastructure development in New Zealand and the long term. Beyond is a digital twin and it's effectively the management layer that allows engineers to manage, access, query and download data out of NCD. Within Beyond, we've configured an AI using Microsoft Foundry, which allows the engineers to use agentic AI to then interface with Beyond period, the underlying database and yet much easier access. To the underlying data, but our measurements, the introduction of the AI has meant that it now takes about 40% less time to access data because the AI streamlines all of the soil and rock blogging records that are really intense. I don't have to look through hundreds and hundreds of data points that are not what I need to be looking at at the time. I'd be lost without it. I don't wanna know a world without it. We can now build better because we have learned from the past and we've got so many more resources to draw from.

Satya, using AI to scale infrastructure resilience is the defining challenge of our decade. But as we move from "faster access" to "autonomous decision-making" in critical sectors like Geotechnical or Financial infrastructure, we must address the Epistemic Gap. A resilient system cannot afford a probabilistic "hallucination" when calculating structural stress or market liquidity. I am building the Deterministic Truth Layer that ensures data integrity at the ingestion level. We apply Renormalization Group (RG) principles to filter noise before the AI even touches the signal. True resilience isn't just about speed; it's about building a Deterministic Substrate where the data is mathematically final.

Satya Nadella, this is a wonderful combination of data and AI! Making data accessible truly lays the foundation for better decisions.

I believe that whether it's geotechnical data or a business's revenue model—the true power lies not in the data itself, but in the full business systems that drive it. Often, founders get bogged down in the data pile, but as I believe, we shouldn't just complete tasks, but rather build systems that drive the business itself and ensure scalable growth. When systems are robust, stress is reduced and decision-making becomes faster.

In your view, will AI in the future simply process data, or will it put the entire business architecture on autopilot? I'm curious to know your thoughts on this.

This is a strong example of what AI should be doing in the real world.

Not replacing engineering judgment, but strengthening it by making critical knowledge more accessible, more usable, and more actionable when real infrastructure decisions are on the line.

That matters because resilient infrastructure is not built from data alone. It is built from the ability of skilled professionals to reach the right information at the right time, understand it in context, and act with greater clarity.

Using Azure, Foundry, and BEYON to unlock the New Zealand Geotechnical Database points toward something bigger than productivity. It points toward a future where AI can help translate complex technical information into faster, better-grounded engineering decisions in environments where the consequences are physical, long-term, and public.

That is a meaningful direction.
